questions.q: What is autoscaling in cloud computing?
options: A process that manually increases instance count when demand rises
A feature that automatically adds or removes compute resources based on demand metrics like CPU utilization
A method to reduce application response time
A security feature that restricts resource access
questions.explanation: Autoscaling automatically adjusts the number of instances based on demand and defined policies, ensuring cost efficiency and performance. Option 0 is manual scaling; option 2 relates to optimization but isn't autoscaling; option 3 is security.

questions.q: What is the difference between on-demand and reserved instances?
options: On-demand instances run faster than reserved instances
On-demand instances are pay-as-you-go; reserved instances require upfront commitment for lower hourly rates
Reserved instances are only for databases, on-demand for compute
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: On-demand pricing charges hourly with no commitment; reserved instances require 1-3 year commitments for 30-70% discounts. Option 0 is false; option 2 is too narrow; option 3 is clearly false.

questions.q: Which pricing model is most cost-effective for workloads with unpredictable demand?
options: Reserved instances
Spot instances
On-demand instances
Dedicated hosts
questions.explanation: On-demand instances are ideal for unpredictable workloads since you pay only for what you use with no commitment. Reserved instances (option 0) require commitment; spot instances (option 1) can be interrupted; dedicated hosts (option 3) are expensive.

questions.q: What are spot instances in AWS?
options: Instances that only accept spotty network connectivity
Unused EC2 capacity offered at discounts, but can be terminated when capacity is needed
Instances that are located at specific geographic spots
Instances that use spot checks to detect network latency
questions.explanation: Spot instances are spare capacity sold at 70-90% discounts but can be interrupted. They suit stateless, fault-tolerant workloads. Option 0 is nonsense; option 2 is incorrect; option 3 is about network monitoring.

questions.q: What is elasticity in cloud computing?
options: The ability to stretch compute resources across multiple data centers
The capacity to automatically scale up or down based on demand, allowing cost optimization
A type of load balancer that distributes traffic
A security measure that limits resource access
questions.explanation: Elasticity is the ability to dynamically scale resources up during peaks and down during troughs, matching capacity to demand. Option 0 is imprecise (not about stretching); option 2 is a tool, not the concept; option 3 is security.

questions.q: When scaling horizontally, what are you doing?
options: Increasing the CPU and memory of a single instance
Adding more instances to distribute load across multiple servers
Compressing data to save storage costs
Moving instances to a different geographic region
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more instances (scaling out). Vertical scaling (option 0) increases instance size. Options 2 and 3 are unrelated to scaling.

questions.q: What is cost optimization in cloud computing?
options: Eliminating all cloud services to reduce expenses
Identifying and implementing strategies to reduce cloud spending while maintaining performance
Paying upfront for all cloud resources for the entire year
Using only the cheapest cloud provider available
questions.explanation: Cost optimization balances performance and expense through strategies like reserved instances, autoscaling, storage tiering, and resource right-sizing. Option 0 is elimination; option 2 is prepayment; option 3 is provider selection, not optimization.

questions.q: What metric commonly triggers autoscaling policies?
options: The number of database queries per second
CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics like request count per instance
The geographic location of users
The encryption strength applied to data
questions.explanation: Autoscaling policies use metrics like CPU, memory, request count, or custom CloudWatch metrics to trigger scaling decisions. Option 0 is too specific; option 2 is for routing, not scaling; option 3 is security, not performance.

questions.q: What is the benefit of using autoscaling groups in AWS?
options: To guarantee zero application downtime
To automatically maintain a desired number of instances and replace unhealthy ones for high availability
To encrypt all traffic between instances
To provide unlimited storage for all instances
questions.explanation: Autoscaling groups maintain desired capacity and replace failed instances, providing self-healing infrastructure and fault tolerance. Option 0 overstates the guarantee; option 2 is encryption; option 3 is storage provisioning.

questions.q: Why might a company choose reserved instances despite their upfront cost?
options: Reserved instances are faster than on-demand
To reduce hourly rates by 30-70% for predictable, long-term workloads and improve budget certainty
Reserved instances provide unlimited scalability
To eliminate the need for security groups
questions.explanation: Reserved instances offer significant discounts for committed capacity, lowering TCO for stable workloads and providing cost predictability. Option 0 is false; option 2 is scalability (related but not the driver); option 3 is unrelated.
